Senti17 at SemEval-2017 Task 4: Ten Convolutional Neural Network Voters for Tweet Polarity Classiﬁcation

Hussam Hamdan

Labex Observatoire de la vie littraire (OBVIL) Laboratoire d’Informatique de Paris 6 (LIP6), Pierre and Marie Curie University,

251ad4c0-dc55-4139-bffc-bbc925c7cb91.label: text_chunk
251ad4c0-dc55-4139-bffc-bbc925c7cb91.text:
UMR 7606 4 place Jussieu, 75005, Paris, France Hussam.Hamdan@lip6.fr

Abstract

This paper presents Senti17 system which uses ten convolutional neural networks (Con- vNet) to assign a sentiment label to a tweet. The network consists of a convolutional layer followed by a fully-connected layer and a Soft- max on top.

15bf7af7-780c-47fa-9937-3ca7eee64f1a.label: text_chunk
15bf7af7-780c-47fa-9937-3ca7eee64f1a.text:
Ten instances of this net- work are initialized with the same word em- beddings as inputs but with different initial- izations for the network weights.

 We com- bine the results of all instances by selecting the sentiment label given by the majority of the ten voters. This system is ranked fourth in SemEval-2017 Task4 over 38 systems with 67.4% average recall.

f28ba3c0-0475-4f0b-aa66-ecb08112a35e.label: text_chunk
f28ba3c0-0475-4f0b-aa66-ecb08112a35e.text:
1 Introduction

Polarity classification is the basic task of sentiment analysis in which the polarity of a given text should be classified into three categories: positive, negative or neutral. In Twitter where the tweet is short and written in informal language, this task needs more attention. SemEval has proposed the task of Mes- sage Polarity Classification in Twitter since 2013, the objective is to classify a tweet into one of the three polarity labels (Rosenthal et al., 2017).

b89aa2ef-dd86-4c14-a786-4a498dda17ef.label: text_chunk
b89aa2ef-dd86-4c14-a786-4a498dda17ef.text: We can remark that in 2013, 2014 and 2015 most best systems were based on a rich feature extraction process with a traditional classifier such as SVM (Mohammad et al., 2013) or Logistic regression (Hamdan et al., 2015). In 2014, Kim (2014) pro- posed to use one convolutional neural network for sentence classification, he fixed the size of the in- put sentence and concatenated its word embeddings

2be880bd-6284-42d3-b62f-860d38ba4f7e.label: text_chunk
2be880bd-6284-42d3-b62f-860d38ba4f7e.text: for representing the sentence, this architecture has been exploited in many later works.

cb7f6cbf-ed1c-4a7e-b4ac-f80788695ce5.label: text_chunk
cb7f6cbf-ed1c-4a7e-b4ac-f80788695ce5.text: Severyn and Moschitti (2015) adapted the convolutional network proposed by Kim (2014) for sentiment analysis in Twitter, their system was ranked second in SemEval- 2015 while the first system (Hagen et al., 2015) com- bined four systems based on feature extraction and the third ranked system used logistic regression with different groups of features (Hamdan et al.

37af9c76-13e8-410e-a8e4-2c1d5253da68.label: text_chunk
37af9c76-13e8-410e-a8e4-2c1d5253da68.text: , 2015).In 2016, we remark that the number of participa- tions which use feature extraction systems were de- graded, and the first four systems used Deep Learn- ing, the majority used a convolutional network ex- cept the fourth one (Amir et al., 2016).

68025b0c-be91-4506-9940-f578ad50634f.label: text_chunk
68025b0c-be91-4506-9940-f578ad50634f.text: Despite of that, using Deep Learning for sentiment analysis in Twitter has not yet shown a big improvement in comparison to feature extraction, the fifth and sixth systems (Hamdan, 2016) in 2016 which were built upon feature extraction process were only (3 and 3.5% respectively) less than the first system. But We think that Deep Learning is a promising direction in sentiment analysis.

6c6e89ff-45d1-46dc-a9ea-2410c06aba33.label: text_chunk
6c6e89ff-45d1-46dc-a9ea-2410c06aba33.text:
Therefore, we proposed to use convolutional networks for Twitter polarity classifi- cation.

Our proposed system consists of a convolutional layer followed by fully connected layer and a soft- max on top. This is inspired by Kim (2014), we just added a fully connected layer. This architec- ture gives a good performance but it could be im- proved. Regarding the best system in 2016 (Deriu et al.

bdd10d30-1c78-4425-82ff-954b14dbfa90.label: text_chunk
bdd10d30-1c78-4425-82ff-954b14dbfa90.text: , 2016), it uses different word embeddings for ini- tialisation then it combines the predictions of differ- ent nets using a meta-classifier, Word2vec and Glove have been used to vary the tweet representation.

99ec67fb-4bee-481a-8864-8f477f61c9c4.label: text_chunk
99ec67fb-4bee-481a-8864-8f477f61c9c4.text: In our work, we propose to vary the neural net- work weights instead of tweet representation which can get the same effect of varying the word embed- dings, therefore we vary the initial weights of the network to produce ten different nets, a voting sys- tem over the these ten voters will decide the senti- ment label for a tweet.

b49c6d4b-bef9-47d9-b013-3cf141d66354.label: text_chunk
b49c6d4b-bef9-47d9-b013-3cf141d66354.text:
The remaining of this paper is organized as fol- lows: Section 2 describes the system architecture, Section 3 presents our experiments and results and Section 4 is devoted for the conclusion.

2 System Architecture

The architecture of our convolutional neural net- work for sentiment classification is shown on Fig. 1.

de4a1d7b-8ce4-413d-8e57-0c3fd10150fd.label: text_chunk
de4a1d7b-8ce4-413d-8e57-0c3fd10150fd.text:
Our network is composed of a single convolu- tional layer followed by a non-linearity, max pool- ing, Dropout, fully connected layer and a soft-max classification layer. Here we describe this architec- ture:

2.1 Tweet Representation

We first tokenize each tweet to get all terms using HappyTokenizer 1 which captures the words, emoti- cons and punctuations.

34dccfe3-0641-4ba7-8944-d137e0efd15f.label: text_chunk
34dccfe3-0641-4ba7-8944-d137e0efd15f.text: We also replace each web link by the term url and each user name by uuser . Then, we used Structured Skip-Gram embeddings (SSG) (Ling et al., 2015) which was compiled by (Amir et al., 2016) using 52 million tweets.

927747ce-db03-4c4b-ad30-5f5a124c2abf.label: text_chunk
927747ce-db03-4c4b-ad30-5f5a124c2abf.text: Each term in the tweet is replaced by its SSG em- bedding which is a vector of d dimensions, all term vectors are concatenated to form the input matrix where the number of rows is d and the number of columns is set to be maxl: the max tweet length in the training dataset. This 2-dim matrix is the input layer for the neural network.

7080700a-4e72-442e-bc93-25575829c3bb.label: text_chunk
7080700a-4e72-442e-bc93-25575829c3bb.text:
2.2 Convolutional Layers

We connect the input matrix with different convolu- tional layers, each one applies a convolution opera- tion between the input matrix and a filter of size m x d .

f4d683fd-db7b-4ffb-89eb-d7a390e26da7.label: text_chunk
f4d683fd-db7b-4ffb-89eb-d7a390e26da7.text:
This is an element-wise operation which cre- ates f vectors of maxl-m+1 dimension where f is the number of filters or feature maps.This layer is supposed to capture the common pat- terns among the training tweets which have the same
filter size but occur at any position of the tweet.

eaddb7eb-510d-4dbd-a97b-a2a4b65bff47.label: text_chunk
eaddb7eb-510d-4dbd-a97b-a2a4b65bff47.text:
To capture the common patterns which have different sizes we have to use more than one layer therefore we defined 8 different layers connected to the input matrix with different filter sizes but the same number of feature maps.

2.3 Activation Layer

Each convolutional layer is typically followed by a non-linear activation function, RELU (Rectified Linear Unit ) layer will apply an element-wise oper- ation to swap the negative numbers to 0.

e5b2427a-6494-4192-a5ef-0637f3de5b5f.label: text_chunk
e5b2427a-6494-4192-a5ef-0637f3de5b5f.text:
The output of a ReLU layer is the same size as the input, just with all the negative values removed. It speeds up the training and is supposed to produce more accu- rate results.

2.4 Max-Pooling Layer

This layer reduces the size of the output of activa- tion layer, for each vector it selects the max value. Different variation of pooling layer can be used: av- erage or k-max pooling.

e88d0c25-d47c-40a9-901c-856d7c942a2d.label: text_chunk
e88d0c25-d47c-40a9-901c-856d7c942a2d.text:
2.5 Dropout Layer

Dropout is used after the max pooling to regularize the ConvNet and prevent overfitting. It assumes that we can still obtain a reasonable classification even when some of the neurones are dropped. Dropout consists in randomly setting a fraction p of input units to 0 at each update during training time.

da96e7e7-1414-4224-abaf-baad241d03db.label: text_chunk
da96e7e7-1414-4224-abaf-baad241d03db.text:
2.6 Fully Conected Layer

We concatenate the results of all pooling layers af- ter applying Dropout, these units are connected to a fully connected layer. This layer performs a ma- trix multiplication between its weights and the input units. A RELU non-linarity is applied on the results of this layer.

2.7 Softmax Layer

The output of the fully connected layer is passed to a Softmax layer.

47498557-7e66-49f3-863e-f09c9a683625.label: text_chunk
47498557-7e66-49f3-863e-f09c9a683625.text:
It computes the probability distri- bution over the labels in order to decide the most probable label for a tweet.

3 Experiments and Results

For training the network, we used about 30000 En- glish tweets provided by SemEval organisers and
1 http://sentiment.christopherpotts.net/tokenizing.html
Figure 1: Network architecture.the test set of 2016 which contains 12000 tweets as development set.

1da15279-4c71-4eb5-9d3b-b1725fcaf783.label: text_chunk
1da15279-4c71-4eb5-9d3b-b1725fcaf783.text:
The test set of 2017 is used to evaluate the system in SemEval-2017 competition. For implementing our system we used python and Keras 2 .

We set the network parameters as follows: SSG embbeding size d is chosen to be 200, the tweet max legnth maxl is 99.

66c3bcf1-4f3a-4310-8118-ff40a440b95a.label: text_chunk
66c3bcf1-4f3a-4310-8118-ff40a440b95a.text:
For convolutional layers, we set the number of feature maps f to 50 and used 8 fil- ter sizes (1,2,3,4,5,2,3,4). The p value of Dropout layer is set to 0.3.

 We used Nadam optimizer (Dozat, 2015) to update the weights of the network and back-propogation algorithm to compute the gradi- ents.

6e22d204-f62c-4f45-b7ea-78f724b3d6bd.label: text_chunk
6e22d204-f62c-4f45-b7ea-78f724b3d6bd.text:
The batch size is set to be 50 and the training data is shuffled after each iteration.

We create ten instances of this network, we ran- domly initialize them using the uniform distribution, we repeat the random initialization for each instance 100 times, then we pick the networks which gives the highest average recall score as it is considered the official measure for system ranking.

7b5f0705-ea39-431f-ac86-114a8d152df0.label: text_chunk
7b5f0705-ea39-431f-ac86-114a8d152df0.text:
If the top network of each instance gives more than 95% of its results identical to another chosen network, we choose the next top networks to make sure that the ten networks are enough different.

Thus, we have ten classifiers, we count the num- ber of classifiers which give the positive, negative and neutral sentiment label to each tweet and select the sentiment label which have the highest number of votes.

7ca679fa-15ef-4239-9856-6c05fe506d3e.label: text_chunk
7ca679fa-15ef-4239-9856-6c05fe506d3e.text:
For each new tweet from the test set, we convert it to 2-dim matrix, if the tweet is longer than
maxl , it will be truncated. We then feed it into the ten networks and pass the results to the voting system.

Official ranking: Our system is ranked fourth over 38 systems in terms of macro-average recall. Table 4 shows the results of our system on the test set of 2016 and 2017.Test Dataset Avg.

4 Conclusion

We presented our deep learning approach to Twitter sentiment analysis.

f5b91685-82b5-4ec4-91d8-e20cb99f982d.label: text_chunk
f5b91685-82b5-4ec4-91d8-e20cb99f982d.text: We used ten convolutional neu- ral network voters to get the polarity of a tweet, each voter has been trained on the same training data us- ing the same word embeddings but different initial weights. The results demonstrate that our system is competitive as it is ranked forth in SemEval-2017 task 4-A.

The relationships involving MedDRA (Medical Dictionary for Regulatory Activities) highlight its critical role in the field of pharmacovigilance and healthcare informatics. 

1. **MedDRA and Adverse Drug Reactions (ADRs)**: MedDRA provides standardized terminology for describing ADRs, which enhances communication and understanding in regulatory contexts. It is specifically utilized for encoding ADRs, ensuring consistency in reporting and analysis.

2. **MedDRA and RNF System**: Data encoded using MedDRA must be transmitted to the RNF (Regulatory Network Framework) system, emphasizing its importance in pharmacovigilance efforts.

3. **MedDRA and Biopharmaceuticals**: MedDRA is instrumental in classifying adverse event information related to biopharmaceuticals, indicating its relevance in the medical terminology landscape.

4. **MedDRA and Selection Criteria**: Section 3.1.5 of MedDRA discusses the criteria for selecting terms, illustrating the structured approach to its terminology development.

5. **MedDRA's Terminology Scope**: MedDRA contains approximately 75,000 distinct terms and about 17,000 unique words, showcasing the extensive and diverse vocabulary it encompasses for medical terminology.

6. **Word Frequency Analysis**: The frequency of terms within MedDRA is analyzed to quantify how often specific words appear, further supporting its role as a comprehensive source of medical language.

In summary, MedDRA serves as a foundational tool in the classification, encoding, and communication of adverse drug reactions and related medical information, facilitating better regulatory practices and healthcare outcomes.

Position embeddings emphasize the sequential order of words, while character-ngram-based embeddings concentrate on the structural aspects of text through character sequences.

2. **Position Embeddings** are derived from **Character N-gram Embeddings**, suggesting that the latter serves as a foundational method for the computation of position embeddings.
